creator: feld@FreeBSD.org
text:
Every port in the ports tree is supposed to produce a package with a
unique name -- ${UNIQUENAME}. This is not 100% implemented yet to my
knowledge, but being worked on.

If you have a dns/bind and games/bind they should declare UNIQUENAME in
their Makefiles so when you "pkg add bind" you get dns/bind and to get
the
games/bind you might have to do "pkg add bind-game"

This is different than a package compiled from a port with non-default
options.

bind99-base is not the bind99-base package, it is the bind99 package
with
the REPLACE_BASE option chosen -- more or less a custom "flavor" of the
bind99 package.
Not all packages add -something to declare their difference from the
normally built package.

My question now is, what package repository are you using? Clearly you
built this package from ports at some point to choose the REPLACE_BASE
option. Did
you point at the official repos and try to upgrade your locally built
packages? The official repos only build packages with the *default*
build
options. What happened was you received an updated version of that
package
built with the default options. It's not possible for a repository to
have
multiple flavors of a package with different options.

Basically, if you select non-default options for your ports your
only choice is to compile from ports on each server you run or to erect
your own private package repository. This is very easy to do with
ports-mgmt/poudriere, and I highly recommend it.

Can you confirm if I'm correct -- you recently tried updating from a
public repository, and previously you had compiled that port yourself
and selected the REPLACE_BASE option?
